color: rgb(10, 10, 10); font-family: &quot;Google Sans&quot;, Roboto, Arial, sans-serif; font-size: 16px; font-style: normal; font-variant-ligatures: normal; font-variant-caps: normal; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: normal; orphans: 2; text-align: left; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; widows: 2; word-spacing: 0px; -webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; white-space: normal; background-color: rgb(255, 255, 255); text-decoration-thickness: initial; text-decoration-style: initial; text-decoration-color: initial;"><b class="Yjhzub" data-complete="true" style="color: rgb(10, 10, 10); font-weight: 500;">Event Loop:</b><span>&nbsp;</span>The event loop acts as the conductor of the JavaScript runtime. It continuously monitors the call stack and the callback queue (and microtask queue). If the call stack is empty, it takes the first callback function from the queue (microtask queue first, then callback queue) and pushes it onto the call stack for execution. This mechanism allows JavaScript, despite being single-threaded, to handle asynchronous operations and provide a non-blocking user experience.</span><ul class="U6u95" data-complete="true" style="margin: 16px 0px; padding: 0px; font-family: &quot;Google Sans&quot;, Roboto, Arial, sans-serif; font-size: 16px; line-height: 24px; padding-inline-start: 16px; color: rgb(10, 10, 10); font-style: normal; font-variant-ligatures: normal; font-variant-caps: normal; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: normal; orphans: 2; text-align: left; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; widows: 2; word-spacing: 0px; -webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; white-space: normal; background-color: rgb(255, 255, 255); text-decoration-thickness: initial; text-decoration-style: initial; text-decoration-color: initial;"><li data-hveid="CAMIABAp" data-complete="true" data-sae="" style="margin: 0px 0px 16px; padding: 0px; list-style: disc; padding-inline-start: 4px;"><span class="T286Pc" data-complete="true" style="overflow-wrap: break-word;"><b class="Yjhzub" data-complete="true" style="color: rgb(10, 10, 10); font-weight: 500;">Call Stack:</b><span>&nbsp;</span>This is where the JavaScript code is actually executed. When a function is called, it's pushed onto the stack, and when it finishes, it's popped off.</span></li></ul>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="430" width="310" x="1130" y="340" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="5oucTEbNXaERr6HkM7WX-22" parent="1" style="text;html=1;align=center;verticalAlign=middle;whiteSpace=wrap;rounded=0;" value="<b>Key components</b>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry works</span>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="40" width="120" x="720" y="930" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="5oucTEbNXaERr6HkM7WX-34" parent="1" style="rounded=0;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;" value="<font style="font-size: 16px;">The JavaScript engine starts by executing the synchronous code</font>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="40" width="690" x="480" y="1009.6" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="5oucTEbNXaERr6HkM7WX-36" parent="1" style="rounded=0;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;" value="<font style="font-size: 16px;">If an asynchronous task (like a setTimeout or a fetch request) is encountered, the JavaScript engine offloads it to the Web APIs (in a browser environment) or to the system's underlying APIs (in environments like Node.js).</font>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="60" width="690" x="480" y="1089.6" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="5oucTEbNXaERr6HkM7WX-37" parent="1" style="rounded=0;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;" value="<font style="font-size: 16px;">The JavaScript engine continues executing the remaining synchronous code</font>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="40" width="690" x="480" y="1169.6" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="5oucTEbNXaERr6HkM7WX-38" parent="1" style="rounded=0;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;" value="<font style="font-size: 16px;">Once the asynchronous task is completed (e.g., the timer for setTimeout runs out, or the fetch request gets a response), the associated callback function is placed in the callback queue (or microtask queue for Promises).</font>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="60" width="690" x="480" y="1249.6" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="5oucTEbNXaERr6HkM7WX-39" parent="1" style="rounded=0;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;" value="<font style="font-size: 16px;">The event loop constantly checks if the call stack is empty.</font>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="50" width="690" x="480" y="1349.6" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="5oucTEbNXaERr6HkM7WX-40" parent="1" style="rounded=0;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;" value="<font style="font-size: 16px;">If the call stack is empty, the event loop takes the first callback from the microtask queue (if any), pushes it onto the call stack, and the JavaScript engine executes it.</font>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="60" width="690" x="480" y="1449.6" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="5oucTEbNXaERr6HkM7WX-41" parent="1" style="rounded=0;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;" value="<font style="font-size: 16px;">After processing all microtasks, the event loop then moves callbacks from the callback queue to the call stack, one at a time, until both queues are empty.&nbsp;</font>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="60" width="690" x="480" y="1539.6" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="5oucTEbNXaERr6HkM7WX-42" parent="1" style="text;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;" value="<span style="color: rgb(10, 10, 10); font-family: &quot;Google Sans&quot;, Roboto, Arial, sans-serif; font-style: normal; font-variant-ligatures: normal; font-variant-caps: normal; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: normal; orphans: 2; text-align: start; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; widows: 2; word-spacing: 0px; none; display: inline !important;"><font style="font-size: 16px;">This continuous cycle of pushing functions onto the call stack for execution and managing callbacks in queues via the event loop is what allows JavaScript to appear to perform multiple tasks concurrently despite its single-threaded nature</font></span>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="80" width="690" x="480" y="1639.6"   1. JavaScript Engine: This is the core component that reads, parses, and executes JavaScript code. It translates the JavaScript code written by developers into machine-readable instructions that the computer's hardware can understand.
    • V8: Developed by Google, powers Chrome and Node.js.
    • SpiderMonkey: Developed by Mozilla, used in Firefox.
    • JavaScriptCore: Used in Safari.

  1. Web APIs (in Browsers): These are functionalities provided by the browser (not the JavaScript language itself) that allow JavaScript to interact with the browser environment and handle tasks like:
    • DOM (Document Object Model): Manipulating HTML and CSS elements.
    • AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML) / Fetch API: Making network requests.
    • Timers: Functions like setTimeout and setInterval.
    • Event Listeners: Handling user interactions like clicks, according to Alisha Shrestha on Medium.
    • Local Storage Interface: Storing data locally in the browser.

li.span: Callback Queue (or Task Queue / Macrotask Queue): This queue holds callback functions that are ready to be executed after asynchronous operations (like network requests, timers, or event listeners) are completed by Web APIs. It operates on a "first-in, first-out" (FIFO) principle.

a.xlink:href:
https://app.diagrams.net/
  1. Microtask Queue: This queue holds callbacks from Promises and queueMicrotask() invocations. according to Dev Community It has higher priority than the callback queue, meaning microtasks are processed before macrotasks.

li.span: Microtask Queue: This queue holds callbacks from Promises and queueMicrotask() invocations. It has higher priority than the callback queue, meaning microtasks are processed before macrotasks.

div.span: Event Loop: The event loop acts as the conductor of the JavaScript runtime. It continuously monitors the call stack and the callback queue (and microtask queue). If the call stack is empty, it takes the first callback function from the queue (microtask queue first, then callback queue) and pushes it onto the call stack for execution. This mechanism allows JavaScript, despite being single-threaded, to handle asynchronous operations and provide a non-blocking user experience.

ul.style: margin: 16px 0px; padding: 0px; font-family: "Google Sans", Roboto, Arial, sans-serif; font-size: 16px; line-height: 24px; padding-inline-start: 16px; color: light-dark(rgb(10, 10, 10), rgb(229, 229, 229)); font-style: normal; font-variant-ligatures: normal; font-variant-caps: normal; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: normal; orphans: 2; text-align: left; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; widows: 2; word-spacing: 0px; -webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; white-space: normal; background-color: light-dark(rgb(255, 255, 255), rgb(18, 18, 18)); text-decoration-thickness: initial; text-decoration-style: initial; text-decoration-color: initial;

span.style: overflow-wrap: break-word;

li.span: Call Stack: This is where the JavaScript code is actually executed. When a function is called, it's pushed onto the stack, and when it finishes, it's popped off.

div.font: The JavaScript engine starts by executing the synchronous code

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 688px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1120px; margin-left: 471px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.font: If an asynchronous task (like a setTimeout or a fetch request) is encountered, the JavaScript engine offloads it to the Web APIs (in a browser environment) or to the system's underlying APIs (in environments like Node.js).

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 688px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1190px; margin-left: 471px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.font: The JavaScript engine continues executing the remaining synchronous code

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 688px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1280px; margin-left: 471px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.font: Once the asynchronous task is completed (e.g., the timer for setTimeout runs out, or the fetch request gets a response), the associated callback function is placed in the callback queue (or microtask queue for Promises).

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 688px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1375px; margin-left: 471px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.font: The event loop constantly checks if the call stack is empty.

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 688px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1480px; margin-left: 471px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.font: If the call stack is empty, the event loop takes the first callback from the microtask queue (if any), pushes it onto the call stack, and the JavaScript engine executes it.

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 688px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1570px; margin-left: 471px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.font: After processing all microtasks, the event loop then moves callbacks from the callback queue to the call stack, one at a time, until both queues are empty.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 688px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1647px; margin-left: 472px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

span.style: color: light-dark(rgb(10, 10, 10), rgb(229, 229, 229)); font-family: "Google Sans", Roboto, Arial, sans-serif; font-style: normal; font-variant-ligatures: normal; font-variant-caps: normal; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: normal; orphans: 2; text-align: start; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; widows: 2; word-spacing: 0px; -webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; white-space: normal; background-color: light-dark(rgb(255, 255, 255), rgb(18, 18, 18)); text-decoration-thickness: initial; text-decoration-style: initial; text-decoration-color: initial; float: none; display: inline !important;
span.font: This continuous cycle of pushing functions onto the call stack for execution and managing callbacks in queues via the event loop is what allows JavaScript to appear to perform multiple tasks concurrently despite its single-threaded nature
